coredata.dc:title: Modeling the electro-chemo-mechanical failure at the lithium-solid electrolyte interface: Void evolution and lithium penetration
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of the Mechanics and Physics of Solids

coredata.dc:description: The solid-solid contact interface is crucial for the reliability of solid-state energy storage systems. The contact condition becomes more complicated when lithium (Li) metal is used as the anode. The contact between solid electrolyte (SE) and Li metal is inferior compared to the liquid/solid interface in conventional Li-ion batteries. Experimental evidence has shown that improper operating conditions of solid-state batteries can lead to electro-chemo-mechanical failures at the Li/SE interface, including the formation of voids and the penetration of Li. In this study, a unified phase-field model is developed to investigate these two mechanisms. The model considers the coupled electro-chemo-mechanical processes including void diffusion, lattice annihilation, stripping and plating reactions, and plastic deformation of Li metal. The study begins with a revisit of the deformation-mechanism map for Li metal under a wide range of temperatures, stress, and deformation rates. This map serves as the basis for the mechanical characterization in the phase-field model. The large inelastic deformation of Li is considered by introducing an advection term into the Allen-Cahn equation, which is used to describe the dynamic evolution of the Li and void phases. The effects of current density and stack pressure on void evolution and Li penetration are studied based on the model predictions. By combining the simulation results with the experimental data from publications, we obtain the stable operation zone of stack pressure and applied current density. In this zone, the Li/SE interface can enable stable stripping and plating of Li metal. The same phase-field modeling framework is transferred to investigate the Li-Mg alloy/SE interface considering Li-Mg alloy is also used as the anode. The fundamental difference between Li/SE and Li-Mg/SE is analyzed accordingly. This study provides a useful tool for the design, manufacturing, and management of next-generation batteries by providing important scientific insights into the electro-chemo-mechanical processes of different anode materials under various operational conditions.

The green area, which is the overlaps of the difference set of the yellow and red area within whole parameter space, is the anticipated area for stable operation.

head.ce:title: Modeling the electro-chemo-mechanical failure at the lithium-solid electrolyte interface: Void evolution and lithium penetration

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The solid-solid contact interface is crucial for the reliability of solid-state energy storage systems. The contact condition becomes more complicated when lithium (Li) metal is used as the anode. The contact between solid electrolyte (SE) and Li metal is inferior compared to the liquid/solid interface in conventional Li-ion batteries. Experimental evidence has shown that improper operating conditions of solid-state batteries can lead to electro-chemo-mechanical failures at the Li/SE interface, including the formation of voids and the penetration of Li. In this study, a unified phase-field model is developed to investigate these two mechanisms. The model considers the coupled electro-chemo-mechanical processes including void diffusion, lattice annihilation, stripping and plating reactions, and plastic deformation of Li metal. The study begins with a revisit of the deformation-mechanism map for Li metal under a wide range of temperatures, stress, and deformation rates. This map serves as the basis for the mechanical characterization in the phase-field model. The large inelastic deformation of Li is considered by introducing an advection term into the Allen-Cahn equation, which is used to describe the dynamic evolution of the Li and void phases. The effects of current density and stack pressure on void evolution and Li penetration are studied based on the model predictions. By combining the simulation results with the experimental data from publications, we obtain the stable operation zone of stack pressure and applied current density. In this zone, the Li/SE interface can enable stable stripping and plating of Li metal. The same phase-field modeling framework is transferred to investigate the Li-Mg alloy/SE interface considering Li-Mg alloy is also used as the anode. The fundamental difference between Li/SE and Li-Mg/SE is analyzed accordingly. This study provides a useful tool for the design, manufacturing, and management of next-generation batteries by providing important scientific insights into the electro-chemo-mechanical processes of different anode materials under various operational conditions.

ce:list-item.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Thanks to the enhanced safety and increased energy density, Li-metal all-solid-state batteries (Li-ASSBs) are considered a promising next-generation alternative to conventional Li-ion batteries (Huo and Janek, 2023). The key to successful Li-ASSBs is engineering a stable and efficient interface between the Li anode and the solid electrolyte (SE). The challenge lies in the complex physics involving the coupled effects of mechanical contact, large deformation, chemical reactions, and transport of mass. During the cyclic operation of Li-ASSBs, Li ions travel through the electrolyte to deposit or dissolve at the Li/SE interface, known as Li plating and stripping, respectively. In the context of liquid electrolyte (LE), the surface of the Li metal layer can be well-wetted. Hence, the alterations in the Li metal geometry resulting from material deposition and dissolution can be effectively compensated by the flow of sufficient LE. This ensures good contact between the LE and Li metal. However, for the Li/SE interface, voids can formulate and grow due to the existence of initial imperfection and the subsequent non-uniform geometrical variation between the SE and Li, eventually causing interfacial contact loss (Krauskopf et al., 2019a, 2019b).

Another important source of interfacial failure at the Li/SE interface is the penetration of Li into the SE, which can lead to an internal short circuit and catastrophic thermal runaway. Early rudimentary models, rooted in the elastic and no-flaw assumption of polymer separators (Monroe and Newman, 2005), posited that increased stiffness could effectively mitigate lithium penetration. Inspired by this work, vast experimental studies on ceramic SEs have been reported (Cao et al., 2023; Doux et al., 2020; Zhao et al., 2023a). However, the Li metal can still penetrate high-stiffness SEs through the grain boundaries (GBs) due to its higher electronic conductivity compared with SE grains (Han et al., 2019). Furthermore, due to the existence of initial imperfections such as pores and cracks in the SE, Li penetration can occur either during plating as a result of continuous reactions or when subjected to high stack pressure as a result of viscoplastic deformation (Cao et al., 2023; Doux et al., 2020; Zhao et al., 2023a). The Li metal can gradually accumulate in the cracks or pores, leading to crack propagation and potentially an internal short circuit (Klinsmann et al., 2019; Ning et al., 2023).

Existing approaches to mitigate the risk of Li penetration and stabilize interfacial contact can generally be classified into two categories. One is developing novel materials with unique material properties that can intrinsically overcome the interfacial issues (Diallo et al., 2024; Hao et al., 2023; Wang et al., 2023). The other is regulating the interfaces of existing materials with system-level management techniques such as controlling the stack pressure and temperature (Doux et al., 2020; Fincher et al., 2022; Lu et al., 2022; Zaman et al., 2023; Zhang et al., 2020). Both categories require a comprehensive understanding of the interplay between electrochemistry and mechanics at the Li/SE interface to develop an electro-chemo-mechanical model. For material development, the model can provide important guidelines; and for system management, it can enable model-based optimization.

The interfacial contact loss at the Li/SE interface can be attributed to the void formation and growth, which is governed by the competing mechanism between the vacancy diffusion within the Li (Krauskopf et al., 2019a, 2019b; Yang and Qi, 2021; Zhao et al., 2022) and the inelastic deformation of the Li (Feng et al., 2022; Lu et al., 2022). During Li stripping, the Li/SE interface experiences the accumulation of vacancies due to diffusion. Excessive current density can lead to void formation if the vacancy concentration near the Li/SE interface surpasses the saturation threshold (Krauskopf et al., 2019a; Schmalzried and Janek, 1998). At elevated stack pressures externally applied on the battery surface, Li metal undergoes large plastic deformation due to its low yield strength (LePage et al., 2019; Narayan and Anand, 2020; Sedlatschek et al., 2021). In this case, the plastic ‘flow’ of Li metal could impede the void development, thus improving the interfacial stability.

Li penetration depends on the electronic conductivity of the grain boundaries in the SE (Han et al., 2019; Tian et al., 2019) and micro/nano-sized cracks at the SE surface (Athanasiou et al., 2024; Fincher et al., 2022; McConohy et al., 2023). The Li penetration caused by the electronic current leaked within the GBs is more likely to occur at high current density, while the Li penetration into the cracks is accelerated at elevated stack pressure. The Li penetration into the cracks at the SE surface is identified as a more significant factor in the micro-scale Li plating experimental study at the microprobe/SE interface (Athanasiou et al., 2024; Fincher et al., 2022; McConohy et al., 2023). The stress of SE near the cracks will increase to a very high level with the accumulation of the penetrated Li metals (Klinsmann et al., 2019), leading to crack propagation due to the wedge-open mechanism until it reaches the cathode side. This process is well captured by the scanning X-ray computed tomography experiment by Ning et al. (2023).

In summary, the stripping and plating processes at the Li/SE interface are dominated by three physical processes—vacancy diffusion, interfacial electrochemical reactions, and plastic deformation of Li metal. Moreover, these processes are coupled and often exhibit competing effects against one another during stripping and plating. In a practical working scenario after manufacturing, the operational condition of a Li-ASSB cell is controlled by adjusting the applied stack pressure on the cell surface, the environmental temperature, and the applied current (density). In this study, we do not consider the effect of temperature and instead focus on investigating the other two variables: stack pressure and applied current. Our aim is to identify an optimal range for stable operation, based on an electro-chemo-mechanical model.

One strategy to model Li penetration and void evolution at the Li/SE interface is the continuum-mechanical method that treats the boundaries as sharp interfaces (Narayan and Anand, 2020; Shishvan et al., 2021, 2023a, 2023b). Narayan and Anand (2020) proposed a continuum-mechanical model in which Li plating and stripping are simplified into the expansion and contraction of a thin-film layer between the Li and SE. The introduced thin-film layer could effectively describe the plating and stripping of Li metal through the continuum-mechanical method without directly modeling the growth of new materials, as the reference configuration changes. Agier et al. (2022) studied void evolution using the arbitrary Lagrange-Euler method and emphasized that the imperfection particles within the Li are the main cause of the void growth and initiation. Shishvan et al. (2023a) proposed a one-dimensional analytical model considering the effect of void diffusion and lattice drift on the void evolution. They found that the void will not grow with no stack pressure due to the lattice drift. This prediction contradicts the recent observations using in-operando scanning electron microscopy (SEM) by Zhao et al. (2023b). In addition, Shishvan et al. (2020b) treated the penetrated Li filament as a climbing edge dislocation to calculate the growth rate of the Li filament at different applied plating current densities analytically, and the critical current density for the Li penetration was obtained. In summary, it is challenging to comprehensively describe the intricate interplay between electrochemical processes and mechanics at the Li/SE interface using the conventional continuum-mechanical method. Simplification and analogies in physics are necessary, such as considering plating/stripping as the expansion/contraction of an artificial thin film (Narayan and Anand, 2020) or analogizing Li penetration to a climbing dislocation (Shishvan et al., 2020b). This makes it difficult to establish a general and unified model for the stripping and plating process at the Li/SE interface.

Another modeling strategy of the Li/SE interface is the phase-field method (Tian et al., 2019; Zhao et al., 2022), which represents the sharp interfacial boundary with a partial differential equation of “order parameters” that governs the evolution of the artificial “phase field”. This strategy has been extensively adopted in analyzing Li plating phenomenon in conventional liquid Li-ion batteries (Chen et al., 2015; Gao and Guo, 2020; Hong and Viswanathan, 2018; Zhang et al., 2021, 2022). Recently, it has been extended to ASSBs. Tian et al. (2019) proposed a phase-field model to study the electrodeposition process of Li in the grain boundary of the SE. In their work, the different SE grains, Li metal, grain boundaries, and cracks are represented by a set of order parameters, and the computational model revealed that the isolated Li deposition within the GBs can be caused by its higher electronic conductivity. Zhao et al. (2022) proposed a thermodynamically consistent phase-field model to study the void evolution during the stripping and plating process, considering the effect of vacancy accumulation at the Li/SE interface. Coupling the SE fracture with Li deposition using the phase-field method, Monismith et al. (2023) studied the effect of SE microstructure on the Li penetration in the lateral direction, which is parallel to the Li/SE interface. Their findings revealed that the direction of Li penetration is significantly affected by lateral stress direction, and that strategically designing the SE microstructure can substantially lower the risk of short circuits caused by Li penetration. Barai et al. (2024) conducted a comprehensive analysis on how surface and bulk lithium diffusivity, stack pressure, and temperature influence void development. They recommend enhancing Li diffusivity through higher operational temperatures and applying external compressive pressure to facilitate Li metal's creep flow, aiming to prevent disconnection at the Li/SE interface. These recent studies highlighted the capability of the phase-field method to effectively capture complex electro-chemo-mechanical interactions respectively for Li penetration and void evolution, shedding light on characterizing the two dominant failure mechanisms in a unified framework.

In this work, we develop an electro-chemo-mechanical phase-field model to simulate Li penetration and void evolution at the Li/SE interface, highlighting two new features compared to existing studies.
(a)
The vacancy diffusion in the Li metal is considered to incorporate lattice annihilation and void formation during stripping.
(b)
The inelastic deformation of Li metal, consisting of the viscoplastic deformation driven by external stack pressure and the deformation caused by the concentration variation of Li atoms and vacancies, is integrated into the kinetical equation of the order parameters through the introduction of an advection term.

Another potential contribution of this work is a comprehensive summary of the mechanical properties of the Li metal under different temperatures and deformation rates, generating a deformation-mechanism map. This map serves as the theoretical basis for the mechanical part of the phase field model.

The manuscript is organized as follows: firstly, the governing equations and boundary conditions of the phase-field model are given including the phase evolution, electrochemical processes in solid electrolyte, and mechanics. The deformation mechanism map for Li metal is concluded. This map serves as the basis for the mechanical aspects of the phase-field model. Secondly, the effect of current density and stack pressure for the void evolution and Li penetration are discussed using the proposed model; thirdly, the stability of the Li/SE interface is discussed based in the parameter space of stack pressure and current density on the simulation results and previous experiments; fourthly, considering that Li-Mg alloy is recently found to be competitive substitution material for the Li metal (Zhao et al., 2023a), the same phase-field modeling framework is extended to model the Li stripping process of interface between Li-Mg alloy and SE, and the mechanical stability of the Li and Li-Mg/SE interface are discussed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Governing equations for phase evolution and vacancy diffusion
ce:section.ce:para:
The free energy of the Li metal phase is contributed by two parts. One is the chemical interaction between the vacancies and Li atoms, and the other is the elastic strain energy which is contributed by the elastic deformation. In this way, the Li metal can be treated as a diffusional solid, in which the Li atoms and vacancies diffuse within the lattice. According to Zhao et al. (2022), the vacancies and Li atoms could be treated as an ideal solution, of which the free energy is calculated in the same way as for ideal gases (Atkins and Paula, 2006). The evolution of order parameters ξ is derived from the mass conservation law:
(20)
∂ ξ
∂ t
+ ∇ ·
(
u ˙
ξ

μ ξ
,
where L is the phase-field mobility and
u ˙
is the speed field of the deformation. The introduction of the velocity field, which is named as advective term, had been widely used in modeling multicomponent fluid flow phenomena (Kim, 2012). In Eq. (20), we consider two main mechanisms: the convection due to Li metal plastic flow and the reaction. The convection term is often ignored in existing literature, which is valid when the deformation of solids is small. For large deformation such as Li metal viscoplastic flow, however, it must be included to describe the mechanical deformation-driven phase evolution. Suppose there is no reaction in a system, the phase would not evolve without the convection term (
∂ ξ / ∂ t = 0
). This advection term is widely used in modeling fluid flow, e.g., the advective Alan-Cahn equation for modeling multiphase flow. It can also be used to model large deformation of solids. For example, the volume of fluid (VOF) method (Hirt and Nichols, 1981) can model mechanical metal forming processes, where the largely deformed metal, as a Eulerian material, is traced with the Eulerian volume fraction (EVF). EVF is one when material fully occupies a location and zero when empty, which can be treated as a phase variable governed by convection.

For Li metal, if it enters the plastic deformation, the viscoplastic flow of Li metal leads to significant change of the geometry of the current configuration of Li metal. In this way, the impact of evolved Li metal geometries on the stripping and plating reactions could be considered.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Deformation mechanism map of lithium metal
ce:section.ce:para:
The mechanical behaviors of Li metal play a key role in both void formation and lithium penetration of Li metal all-solid-state batteries. As a monovalent alkali metal with a body-centered cubic structure, Li metal shows typical elastoplastic behaviors. This paper is mostly concerned with the plastic behaviors considering the large deformation during the manufacturing and operation of Li-ASSBs. Due to a low melting temperature of 453 K, Li metal has three major plastic deformation mechanisms: the plastic flow by dislocation glide, the power-law creep by dislocation climb, and diffusional creep by vacancy diffusion through bulk and boundary. Each of the mechanisms dominates a range of mechanical conditions depending on the applied stress level and temperature. To fully understand its plastic behaviors, we construct a deformation-mechanism map of the lithium metal following the method proposed by Frost and Ashby (1980). Fig. 2 shows the shear strain rate
γ ˙
at different temperature T and shear stress levels
σ s
, and the parameters used in the calculation is shown in Table 1 . The diagram has four regions: lattice-diffusion creep (Nabarro creep), boundary-diffusion creep (Cobel creep), power-law creep, and dislocation-glide plasticity. At low stress, diffusional creep dominates and can be described by
(33)

Note that the deformation-mechanism map describes the steady-state responses. It does not consider the influence of strain-hardening and transient behaviors. During the operation of ASSBs, a high pressure is often applied; the deformation of lithium metal is therefore more likely to be controlled by the power-law creep and dislocation-glide plasticity. The viscoplasticity model proposed by Narayan and Anand (2018) successfully predicts the large deformation of lithium metal in a wide range of temperatures and applied stress (or loading speed). Therefore, we follow Narayan and Anand's viscoplasticity (Narayan and Anand, 2018) model to describe the mechanical behavior of Li metal. Another important point to note is that the current theory of deformation-mechanism maps only partially accounts for microstructural influences by incorporating two parameters: grain size (d) and grain boundary size (δ). These parameters affect diffusional creep. However, the theory does not consider how microstructure impacts slip-controlled plasticity and power-law creep. During continuous stripping and plating, the microstructure at the interface between Li metal and the solid electrolyte (SE) may change and deviate from that of the bulk material. This dynamic microstructural variation can lead to changes in the dominant areas of different creep and plastic deformation mechanisms, necessitating calibration and adjustment for real-world applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Simulation geometry, initial and boundary conditions
ce:section.ce:para:
The geometry used in the simulation and their boundary conditions are shown in Fig. 3 . The void in the Li metal and the cracks in the solid electrolyte are assumed to be rectangle shaped. The height of the void in the metal and the width of the void in the SE are defined as W . The height of the void is set as
1
μ m
, while the width of the crack in the SE is set as

μ m
in the simulation. The width of the void in the metal during stripping and the depth of the void in the SE during plating is defined as L , which is set as
5
μ m
. The symmetric boundary conditions are applied for the upper and lower boundary conditions, namely the nominal displacement, the flux of charge carriers in the SE, and the flux of Li atoms in the lithium metal are zero:

The parameters used in the phase field model are divided into three parts: the parameters for phase evolution equations, the parameters for the electrochemical process in SE, and the parameters for the viscoplastic model of Li, as shown in Table 2 . It is worth mentioning that side reactions exist in sulfide-based electrolytes when contacting with Li. In this study, we demonstrate the theoretical framework to capture the main electro-chemo-mechanical process. Therefore, we choose LLZO as the exemplary SE material. In addition to the three parameters L , ω and κ in the equations of phase evolution, which are often adjusted based on the experimental observation, the important parameters are obtained from previous publications as listed in Table 2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Results: pressure and current effect on void evolution and dendrite penetration
ce:section.ce:para:
In this section, the effect of pressure and current density on the void evolution and Li penetration into the crack is analyzed using the proposed model. For the study of void evolution, simulations are performed at two distinct current density values:
1
A /
m
2
and
5
A /
m
2
, along with three stack pressure levels—namely, zero, 0.1 MPa, and 1 MPa. In simulating Li penetration, the current density is specified at two values:
1
A /
m
2
and
5
A /
m
2
. Additionally, the pressure conditions are defined at 1 MPa and 5 MPa, respectively.

The simulation results of void evolution at different current densities and pressures are shown in Figs. 4–7
. In the SE, the distribution of ionic flux is characterized by the ratio between the ionic current by Li+
i
L
i
+
, which is expressed as
i
L
i
+
= F
N
L
i
+
, and the applied current density
i app
. Fig. 4 could be thought of as the reference where there is no stack pressure. The void transforms from a rectangle shape to a trapezoid shape due to the current concentration at the periphery of the void, which is the three-phase intersection point. At around 0.3 mAh/cm2, the prominent decrease of order parameter ξ at the edge of the void could be observed due to the continuous reduction of Li atoms during stripping. In addition, the relative ionic current in the SE peaks at the void periphery with a value of 3.5 times that of the applied current, as shown in Fig. 4(a)–(d).

With slightly increasing the pressure to 0.1 MPa, the shape evolution of the void and the ionic current distribution in the SE are very similar to that with no pressure adopted, as shown in Fig. S1. This is because Li only experiences elastic deformation at this pressure level.

When the stack pressure is increased to 1 MPa, the void transforms to an M shape due to the creep deformation of the Li metal and shows apparent trends of void break-up, indicating the viscoplastic deformation of Li metal is important for the stabilization of the Li/SE interface. The results are consistent with the results in Zhao et al. (2022), which also shows the void coalescence in the condition of 1 MPa. This case study serves as a validation of our modeling framework.

When the current density increases to
5
A
/
m
2
, apparent void phase is found to form at the area near the void at around 0.15 mAh/cm2 when no pressure or low pressure (0.1 MPa) is applied, as shown in Figs. 6 and S2, indicating that the interface between the Li and SE is nonstable in this case since the diffusion rate of Li atoms in the Li metal could not supplement the removal rate when the current increases to
5
A
/
m
2
, as discussed in Krauskopf et al. (2019a)’s work. As shown in Fig. 6 (c), (d), the void phase starts to grow and expand along the Li/SE interface, indicating there is an apparent trend for the interface detachment when the stripping capacity exceeds 0.125 mAh/cm2. When the pressure is increased to 1 MPa, the central part of the void is bent towards the SE surface due to the viscoplastic deformation of Li, and the void growth at Li/SE is significantly suppressed even until the stripping capacity reaches 0.3 mAh/cm2, while the apparent void formation is already observed at low pressure when the stripping capacity only reaches to 0.15 mAh/cm2. In addition, the low- ξ area near the void periphery is found to be squeezed compared with the case with no pressure and 0.1 MPa pressure, thanks to the plastic flow of the Li metal.

In summary, the effect of current density and stack pressure on the void evolution during the stripping process is explored in this section. At higher current density, the void formation will be accelerated due to the reaction concentration at the void periphery. The viscoplastic deformation of Li metal is found to effectively suppress the void formation, which requires pressing the Li with a stack pressure of 1 MPa.

In this section, the effect of pressure and current density on Li penetration into the cracks in the SE are discussed. Figs. 8–10
presents the Li penetration at different stack pressures. At low pressure (0.1 and 1 MPa), the penetrated part of Li metal in the crack features a rectangle shape and the penetration depth is around
0.15
μ m
when the plated capacity reaches 0.06 mAh/cm2, as shown in Figs. 8 and S3. When the pressure increases to 2.5 MPa, the penetrated part illustrates a round-shape profile and the penetration length reaches
0.3
μ m
when the plated capacity reaches only 0.03 mAh/cm2, as shown in Fig. 9(d). When the stack pressure increases to 5 MPa, the penetrated Li illustrates a trapezoid-shaped profile with a rounded central portion and steps on its leg, as shown in Fig. 10. In addition, the penetration depth of the Li reaches
0.6
μ m
even though the plated capacity is only
7.5
×
10
− 3
mAh/cm2. At a similar period, the penetration depth at 1 MPa and 2.5 MPa are
0.05
μ m
and
0.15
μ m
respectively, indicating the viscoplastic deformation of Li metal dominates the penetration process under high stack pressure.

At the stack pressure with 0.1 MPa, since the applied stack pressure is lower than the measured yield strength (LePage et al., 2019), the penetrated lithium is in an elastic state. Therefore, the penetrated Li is produced from the plating reaction in this condition, and the penetration process at 0.1 MPa is merely contributed by the plating reaction. Taking the penetration depth at 0.1 MPa as the reference, the effect of the plastic deformation on the Li penetration is calculated, and the results are shown in Fig. 11 . The area of the penetrated Li is used to characterize the amount of Li penetration in the crack. The difference between the Li area at higher pressure (higher than 1 MPa) and the penetration area at 0.1 MPa is assumed as the penetration driven by the plastic deformation of Li. The relative penetration area is defined as the ratio between the area driven by the plastic deformation to the overall penetration area at the end of plating. As shown in Fig. 11(a), the penetration process is accelerated significantly when the stack pressure increases. The relative penetration area driven by plastic deformation, which quantifies the contribution of plastic deformation to the Li penetration, is shown in Fig. 11(b). When the stack pressure surpasses 1 MPa, namely the yield strength of Li, the relative area of plastic deformation is over 90 %, indicating that the Li penetration is mainly contributed by the plastic deformation in this condition.

To explore the effect of current density, the penetration process at 1 MPa but with higher current density (2.5 mA/cm2) is also simulated, as shown in Fig. S4. Compared with the case at lower current density, namely Figs. 8 and S3, the profile of the penetrated Li is also a rectangle and the penetration depth at the same plating capacity is the same as that at lower current density. This shows that the current density has a much more trivial effect on the Li penetration process than the stack pressure.

In summary, our simulations of the Li penetration process under varying conditions reveal that the stack pressure significantly impacts the penetration process, exerting a more pronounced influence than the plating reaction when surpassing the yield strength of the material. The plastic deformation of Li becomes the predominant factor in driving penetration at higher stack pressures, accounting for over 90 % of the overall penetration area. On the other hand, our investigation indicates that current density has a comparatively minor effect on the Li penetration process into existing cracks, with the profile and penetration depth remaining consistent even at higher current densities. The current model exclusively accounts for Li penetration within preexisting cracks with an idealized shape. However, it is noteworthy that Li penetration may be influenced by the electronic conductivity at grain boundaries (Tian et al., 2019) and the intricate microstructure of the SE (Singh et al., 2022). A more sophisticated model is required for a systematic exploration of the impact of current density on Li penetration. This aspect will be thoroughly investigated in our upcoming work.

ce:section.ce:section-title: A stability phase diagram for the Li/SE interface
ce:section.ce:para: The simulation findings of this study reveal that the processes of plating and stripping have distinct reliance on the stack pressure and current density. During stripping, an intricate balance is observed between stack pressure and current density: elevated stack pressure mitigates void formation, while increased current densities may intensify void expansion. Conversely, in the plating process, the role of stack pressure is more pronounced, directing Li infiltration into cracks via the viscoplastic deformation of the Li metal, whereas current density has a lesser influence. This observation highlights the critical need for a stability diagram that identifies the ideal stack pressure and current density conditions for maintaining a stable Li-solid electrolyte (SE) interface. The establishment of such a diagram would facilitate the identification of a ‘stable operation zone', i.e., the range of optimal conditions to minimize both void formation and Li penetration, thereby enhancing the design and functionality of Li-ASSBs. Ideally, this diagram should be obtained experimentally. However, there is a gap in experimental research, and as a result, no comprehensive operational guidelines have been formulated for Li-ASSBs to define this essential stable operation zone.

Utilizing the principles derived above, we assessed the interfacial stability under various operational conditions through simulations with our phase-field model. For the stripping process, the Li/SE interface is considered stable when void growth is either suppressed (as demonstrated in Fig. 5, with a stack pressure of 1 MPa and current density of 1 A/m2) or not significantly boosted (as observed in Figs. 4 and S1, at a stack pressure of 0.1 MPa and current density of 1 A/m2). Conversely, rapid void expansion and a clear trend towards interface disconnection denote an unstable Li/SE interface. In the context of plating, the interface is deemed stable when Li penetration is gradual (as depicted in Figs. 8, S3 and 9, with stack pressure of 0.1, 1 MPa and 2.5 MPa respectively). However, if Li penetration is notably accelerated (as shown in Fig. 10), the Li/SE interface is assumed to be unstable.

The phase diagram is shown in Fig. 12 . In addition to our simulation findings, we collected and integrated experimental data from Doux et al. (2020), Fuchs et al. (2023) and Wang et al. (2019b) . The experiments performed by Doux et al. (2020) delineate the boundary for mechanically induced short circuits, marked by two red vertical dashed lines in Fig. 12. In that study, a Li/SE/Li asymmetric cell was subjected to cycling under various stack pressures at a current density of
0.75
A /
m
2
. This low current density setting was chosen to minimize the influence of electrochemical processes, focusing on short circuits caused solely by viscoplastic deformation of Li metal, here referred to as mechanical short circuits. The authors found that short circuits occurred with a zero applied current when the stack pressure exceeded 75 MPa. In the phase diagram, this limit pressure is represented as the critical threshold for immediate mechanical short circuits. Conversely, the cell remained stable when the stack pressure was reduced to 5 MPa. Accordingly, the zone of stable operation is identified to the left of the 5 MPa vertical line, indicating a lower risk of mechanical short circuits within this pressure range.

Fuchs et al. (2023) also explored the critical current density (CCD) for SE by incrementally increasing the current density at a stack pressure of 5 MPa. CCD represents the maximum current density threshold beyond which short circuits are triggered by Li penetration, a phenomenon directly tied to the SE material's transport properties. At this specified stack pressure, void growth at the Li/SE interface of the counter electrode—which undergoes stripping—is mitigated, while not leading to the mechanical short circuit. Consequently, short circuits that do occur are attributed to Li penetration caused by constrained transport within the SE, a scenario identified as an electrochemical short circuit. When current density exceeds the CCD, pronounced Li penetration destabilizes the Li/SE interface. Therefore, the region below the CCD value's horizontal line is considered the stable operational zone, where the risk of electrochemical short circuits is minimized.

Wang et al. (2019b) conducted a study on the Li/solid electrolyte (SE) interface by varying current densities and stack pressures during the stripping of Li metal. Their findings revealed that increasing the pressure permits a higher current density for stable stripping, aligning with the outcomes of our simulations. Consequently, the Li/SE interface stripping process becomes unstable under conditions of low stack pressure combined with high current density. This scenario corresponds to the upper left quadrant of the operational parameter space, depicted as the yellow-colored area in Fig. 12.

Integrating the stable zones identified through the mechanisms discussed above, the intersection of these zones, highlighted in green, delineates the stable area for the Li/SE interface during both stripping and plating processes. This intersection forms a right trapezoid shape. In conditions of very low stack pressure coupled with high current density, the stable zone narrows into a slender strip, demanding precise management of both pressure and current for stable battery operation. Given the intricate physical dynamics at the Li/SE interface, operating within this slim margin poses a risk of instability. Consequently, the optimal operational area is suggested to be the lower right section of the stable zone, where stack pressures range from 1 to 5 MPa and current densities are maintained below 5 A/m2. This region, resembling a cube-like shape, offers greater operational robustness compared to areas characterized by low stack pressures and high currents.

It is important to note that the stability phase diagram presented in this study offers only a semi-quantitative guideline for selecting stack pressure and current density levels. As more physical phenomena are considered, adjustments to the stable zone may be necessary. For instance, at higher current densities, the propensity for electronic current leakage along grain boundaries may set a lower limit for current density, below the previously defined CCD. Diallo et al. (2024) identified a non-monotonic relationship between the pellet density of the solid electrolyte (SE) and Li penetration dynamics. Increased pressure during manufacturing compacts the SE, enhancing its density. This compaction decreases pore size within the SE, potentially hastening Li penetration as less Li metal is required to breach the SE and trigger a short circuit. Beyond a certain density threshold, the pores of SE start to diminish and become isolated, which can inhibit Li penetration and contribute to interface stabilization. Consequently, the phase diagram's applicability may vary with different SE pellet densities, indicating that the diagram will need adjustments based on the specific characteristics of the SE material used.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Interface stability of Li metal and Li alloy during stripping
ce:section.ce:para: Compared with Li metal, the void formation and penetration of Li alloys are less studied. Among various alloy materials, lithium magnesium (Li-Mg) alloy is becoming a competitive substitutional material for lithium metal due to its high solubility range with lithium metal and better stability with solid electrolytes (Krauskopf et al., 2019b; Yang et al., 2019; Zhao et al., 2023a). The work by Krauskopf et al. (2019a) systematically compared the interfacial stability of Li0.9Mg0.1 alloy and the Li using electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). In their work, the EIS of lithium metal and Li0.9Mg0.1 alloy during long-period stripping and relaxation processes are measured. During the long-period stripping, the interfacial resistance of both Li and Li0.9Mg0.1 alloy continuously increases with a clear decrease in the capacitance of the electrochemical double layer (EDL). In the subsequent relaxation process, the impedance for Li remained nearly constant, suggesting no recovery of the contact area at the Li/ SE interface. In contrast, the interface resistance of Li0.9Mg0.1 alloy visibly decreased, indicating a significant recovery of the contact area. Notably, Zhao et al. (2023b) use operando SEM to monitor the interface evolution of Li0.9Mg0.1 alloy and the Li. They found the void in the Li0.9Mg0.1 alloy could break up even though nearly no pressure is exerted on the assembled battery. In contrast, voids at the Li/SE interface tended to grow, coalesce, and ultimately lead to interface failure.

The distinct phenomenon observed at the Li/SE and Li-Mg/SE interfaces arises from variations in their respective microscopic lattice structures. In the case of the Li-Mg alloy, the unreacted magnesium atoms act as a framework facilitating the diffusion of lithium (Li) atoms during stripping. The geometry change at the Li-Mg/SE interface is the deformation by the concentration variation of Li atoms within the Li-Mg alloy, while that of the Li is the material removal resulting from the lattice annihilation. This is well inconsistent with the geometry recovery phenomenon mentioned in the relaxation process after stripping in the work by Krauskopf et al. (2019b). Also, this hypothesis finds support in optical images from prior studies (Feng et al., 2022; Krauskopf et al., 2019a, 2019b; Lu et al., 2022), where a substantial portion of Li foil disappeared, yet the geometric structure of the Li-Mg alloy remained intact after the stripping process.

Based on the mechanism mentioned above, we modify the governing equations, in which the vacancy effect is ignored considering that the stripping of lithium atoms for Li0.9Mg0.1 alloy is considered as the concentration depletion of the lattice instead of lattice annihilation in the lithium metal. This effect appears as a different kinetic equation of order parameters, in which the reaction term is replaced by the conservative Cahn-Hillard equation (Cahn, 1961) considering the lattice annihilation effect is suppressed by the magnesium atoms. The governing equations and parameters are documented in Appendix A and readers could refer to them for further details.

The simulated stripping process of Li-Mg alloy is shown in Fig. 13 . The initial geometry parameters are the same as that of Li and the stack pressure is zero. Compared with the stripping process of Li at no-pressure conditions as shown in Fig. 6, the LiMg alloy remains well in contact with the SE even after the stripping capacity reaches 0.3 mAh/cm2. In addition, the central part of the voids in Li-Mg alloy is moving towards the SE interface and shows an apparent trend of void ‘break-up’. The results are consistent with the operando experimental results in the work by Zhao et al. (2023b).

In this work, a phase-field electro-chemo-mechanical model is proposed, in which the coupling of void diffusion, lattice annihilation, stripping and plating reactions, and viscoplastic deformation of lithium metal are comprehensively described. The generalized Allen-Cahn equation is used to describe the phase evolution, in which the velocity field of deformation is complemented to incorporate the effect of plastic flow and expansion/contraction due to the diffusion of Li atoms and vacancy in the Li. In this way, the current configuration of the deformed lithium metal could be directly reflected by the spatial distribution of the order parameters, and the influence of the varied geometry of lithium metal on the stripping/plating reaction could be described. To comprehensively incorporate the mechanical properties of lithium metals in the model, the deformation mechanism of lithium metals under different temperatures and deformation rates is concluded, which is called the deformation mechanism map and serves as the theoretical basis for the mechanical part of the model. Based on the proposed phase field model, the effect of two working conditions, namely the current density and stack pressure, are analyzed. In addition, the interfacial stability of lithium metal and lithium magnesium alloys is also discussed by modifying the proposed phase-field model to simulate the Li-Mg alloy. The key contributions of this work are summarized below:
(a)
A comprehensive deformation mechanism map of the lithium metal, in which the phase diagram of the deformation mechanism of lithium metal at different shear stresses and temperatures is proposed. This deformation map offers a comprehensive overview of the deformation behavior of lithium metal.
(b)
The influence of the applied current density and stack pressure on the void evolution and lithium penetration into the solid electrolyte is discussed. After the stack pressure surpasses the yield strength, the void will collapse and the contact between the lithium metal and solid electrolyte could be stabilized. However, when the stack pressure is too high (5 times the yield strength), the lithium penetration into the cracks or pores of the solid electrolyte is promoted. Supplemented with experimental results from previous works, the stability of Li/SE interface in the parameter space of stack pressure and current density is discussed. The recommended operation range for stack pressure is from 1 to 5 MPa and that of the current density is lower than 5
A
/
m
2
.
(c)
Different interface stability of lithium magnesium alloy and lithium metal are discussed using the phase field model. The magnesium atoms in the Li-Mg alloys play a role in the frame and the deformation of Li-Mg is caused by the deformation caused by composition change of the Li alloy. The governing equations for the phase field model of Li-Mg alloy are modified, in which the vacancy effect is ignored considering that the stripping of lithium atoms for Li0.9Mg0.1 alloy is closer to concentration depletion of the lattice instead of lattice annihilation in the lithium metal. The simulation results show that the central part of the voids in Li-Mg alloy is moving towards the SE interface and shows an apparent trend of void ‘break-up’ after a similar amount of stripping.

The general phase-field modeling framework proposed in this work can simulate the void evolution and penetration at different operation conditions considering the complex electro-chemo-mechanical effects for Li metal or other Li alloy anode materials, which is believed to be a powerful tool for the effective design, manufacturing, and management of next-generation batteries.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Declaration of generative AI in scientific writing
ce:section.ce:para: During the preparation of this work, the authors did not use generative AI in scientific writing.
Data used in this work is available upon request. Please contact the corresponding author for the data and models.

The difference between the Li alloy and Li metal is that the unreactive atoms in the alloy could offer the frame for the Li atom diffusion and material deformation. Thus, the stripping and plating of the Li metal corresponds to the addition and removal of Li lattice from the Li metal, while that of Li alloy corresponds to the concentration change of the Li atoms in the Li alloy and the lattice structure will remain after stripping.
